questions.question: Define the following: (a) Tuple (b) Degree (c) Cardinality (d) Domain
keyPoints: Tuple: A single row/record in a relation representing one entity instance
Degree: Number of attributes/columns in a relation
Cardinality: Number of tuples/rows in a relation at a given time
Domain: Set of permissible values that an attribute can take

questions.question: Explain the differences between Primary Key, Candidate Key, and Super Key with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Super Key: Any set of attributes that uniquely identifies tuples (e.g., {ChapterID, Title} in Chapters)
Candidate Key: Minimal super key with no proper subset being a key (e.g., {ChapterID} is candidate key)
Primary Key: Chosen candidate key used to identify tuples uniquely (e.g., ChapterID as PK)
Candidate keys can become primary key when selected; primary key cannot be NULL

questions.question: What are the three main integrity rules in the relational model? Explain each briefly.
keyPoints: Entity Integrity: Primary key must not contain NULL values
Referential Integrity: Foreign key must either match a primary key value in the referenced relation or be NULL
Domain Integrity: Attribute values must be atomic and from the defined domain

questions.question: Explain why NULL is not the same as zero or empty string. Give an example scenario.
keyPoints: NULL represents missing or unknown information, not a value
Zero is a numeric value meaning 'no quantity'; NULL means 'value is unknown'
Empty string is a text value of length zero; NULL means 'no value was provided'
Example: If chapter publication date is unknown, it is NULL, not '0' or ''

questions.question: Define relational schema and relational instance. How do they differ?
keyPoints: Relational Schema: Structure definition - relation name, attributes and their domains (e.g., Chapters(ChapterID:int, Title:varchar, Pages:int))
Relational Instance: Actual data/tuples in the relation at a given moment
Schema is static/structure; instance is dynamic/data that changes over time

questions.question: What is a foreign key constraint? Explain its importance with an example from Chapters and Books tables.
keyPoints: Foreign key is an attribute in one relation that references primary key of another
Ensures referential integrity between related tables
Example: Chapters.BookID references Books.BookID
Every BookID in Chapters must either exist in Books or be NULL; prevents orphaned chapter records

questions.question: Differentiate between unary and binary relational operations. Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Unary operations work on a single relation: Selection (σ), Projection (π), Rename (ρ)
Binary operations work on two relations: Union (∪), Set Difference (-), Join (⋈), Cartesian Product (×)
Example unary: σ(Pages>50)(Chapters) - selects chapters with more than 50 pages
Example binary: Chapters ⋈ Books - combines chapters with their book information

questions.question: Define the relational model. Explain its advantages over earlier database models like hierarchical and network models.
keyPoints: Relational model, introduced by E.F. Codd in 1970, represents data as tables (relations) with rows and columns
Data independence: logical schema separated from physical storage
Set-oriented operations: can manipulate entire sets of tuples, not just individual records
Foundation for SQL, the standard database language
No physical pointers: relationships represented by values, not addresses
Theoretical foundation: based on mathematical set theory and predicate logic
Easier query formulation using high-level languages compared to navigation in network/hierarchical

questions.question: Consider a relational database for a library management system with relations: Books(BookID, Title, Author, Publisher), Chapters(ChapterID, ChapterTitle, Pages, BookID). Answer the following: (a) Identify primary keys and foreign keys. (b) If we insert a chapter with BookID=999, what integrity constraint is violated? (c) Can ChapterTitle be NULL? Explain with reasons.
keyPoints: (a) Books: BookID is primary key; Chapters: ChapterID is primary key, BookID is foreign key referencing Books
(b) Referential integrity violated because BookID=999 does not exist in Books table
(c) ChapterTitle can be NULL if not mandatory; but if Title is part of candidate key or required field, cannot be NULL; depends on domain constraints defined

questions.question: Explain the following relational algebra operations with examples using Chapters relation: (a) Selection (σ) (b) Projection (π) (c) Join (⋈)
keyPoints: Selection (σ): Extracts specific rows/tuples based on condition. Example: σ(Pages>30)(Chapters) returns chapters with more than 30 pages
Projection (π): Extracts specific columns/attributes. Example: π(ChapterTitle, Pages)(Chapters) returns only chapter titles and page counts
Join (⋈): Combines tuples from two relations based on common attributes. Example: Chapters ⋈ Books combines each chapter with its book details where Chapters.BookID = Books.BookID
These operations form the theoretical basis for SQL queries

questions.question: Explain the concept of keys in relational model. Discuss different types of keys with appropriate examples from a database containing Student(StudentID, Name, Email, Phone, CourseID) relation.
keyPoints: Super Key: Any attribute combination that uniquely identifies tuples - {StudentID}, {StudentID, Name}, {Email}, {StudentID, Email} all are super keys
Candidate Key: Minimal super key - {StudentID} and {Email} are candidate keys (no proper subset is a key)
Primary Key: Selected candidate key - choose StudentID or Email as primary key
Foreign Key: CourseID references Course table's primary key, establishing relationship
Candidate keys must satisfy: uniqueness (no two tuples have same values) and irreducibility (no attribute can be removed)
